speaker: interviewer
text: We're on tape three, with Rachel Warszauer Schlufman, August 29, 1995. Mrs. Schlufman, you were telling me about your brothers and sisters. I want you to tell me a little bit about Moishe and Sam.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were the two brothers were born after me. Moishe and Sam-- and Shmulek, which we called him then. Now, we call him Sam. They were my favorite two brothers, of course. We grew up together-- the three of us in the wintertime. My brother Moishe was such a talented little boy. He was just wonderful. He was very capable. Brilliant in school, specially in math. He was-- and beside, we had to do homework in the school. Like, girls had to crochet, boys had to do, like, anything to make from wood or something. My brother was so capable, Moishele, that he always worked to make his own sled in the winter. And he worked for weeks on that sled. And we used to have so much fun going in the winter with that sled, all three of us, me and Moishele and Shmulekl. That was so much fun.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, of course, the years went by, you know, very quickly. No. We grew up very fast, you know? And we wanted to grow up fast. Then what happened? Like I talked before, the war-- what happened when people talked about the war, you know? The horrible thing was happening.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 15. Moishele was 13. He was 18 months younger than me. And Shmulek, of course, was 11-- was a kid. 10 and a half-- something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you like me to tell you about the war-- how it was? Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's very difficult for me to explain how the war was. It's painful, but I clearly remember every-- everything, every movement. That was on September-- will be September the 3 or the 4-- when Hitler declared war the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The year?

speaker: interviewee
text: --September 1, 1939. We sat in the house very quietly. We're expecting the Germans to come in. We didn't move out. We were afraid even to look out from a window. We sat and shivered. My father was sitting with his head down and reading the Torah-- reading the Talmud, you know, studying-- reading the Talmud, praying to God that the Germans should come in-- quietly, at least-- and nobody should be hurt. Of course, my parents, in their heart, they knew the First World War. But me, as I mentioned before, I was always on the lookout and being very interested in everything. I wanted to know what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: I sneaked out from the house. I told my parents, I want to see. You know, we had two big doors that closed at night. But I could see through this-- the open-- the openings, you know? I could see what was doing in the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: The city was-- we had-- it was surrounded, all around houses. It was like a mark. It was surrounded all around and streets were leading like-- ah, about 15 streets were reading-- leading to-- you could go to other city, from city to city. But most of the Jewish people lived together in that surrounding where the city was surrounded, that shtetl, all around. So I was looking out what was doing. While I looked out, it was about 11:00-- it was 11 o'clock. I did see--

speaker: interviewer
text: 11 o'clock at night? Or, in the morning?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the morning. In the morning. The sun was so bright. September weather-- the most beautiful weather. And that was before the holidays, Rosh Hashanah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two-- on a motorcycle, two German patrols with dusty helmets-- I could see their faces. If I-- if I talk about them, they stopped that motorcycle. They got off, you know? And they cleaned themselves off from the dust.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took out some instruments. I don't know what they were holding in their hands and going all around to some of the houses and spraying, perhaps with gasoline. And in minutes, I did see a flame busting out from a house. I came in running to my parents. And I says, I saw a flame coming out from a house! The two patrolmen going from one house to the other and spraying, and the flame comes out. As we look out from the window, we see flames. Flames-- dark flames! We picked ourselves up. Everything what we had, we put it in the basement. We weren't afraid for fires. We didn't expect that our home should be burned down. We expected the Germans will come in-- they will rob. They were looking for gold, for silver. They should rob the houses, but not to burn. And, in minutes, all the neighbors was standing and shivering. And everybody put their belongings in the basement. And it was a gate-- a high gate-- going through a garden. We ran through that gate. Everybody jumped over. And we were running next to a lake, where it's a water, you know, to be-- you know, a water. Because we couldn't stay here. We ran, we walked. We walked about, I will say, a mile, until we got to the lake. My mother was so [? aware, ?] the only thing what she remembered to take candlesticks with the challah. Because it was on a Friday. She baked the challah a night before. And most of the people were carrying bread-- bread with candlesticks. That was because it was on Friday, I think. But my mother remembered the candlesticks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and we walked and walked. And I says, why are you carrying-- I says to my mother, why are you carrying the candlesticks? She says, Rachela, wherever we're going to be-- she thought we will get into another house-- that we will hide. We will burn.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Friday night. She was thinking that she wouldn't be able to get back before Shabbas. It's too late. She will be able to light these candles somebody else's house. Go figure it this out. I never thought what my mother was thinking. We walked and walked. We saw a lake. All of the neighbors, we sat-- we sat down. Watching these flames from one mile away, the loud cracking from the glass was unbelievable. The smoke was so black. Waves of smoke were coming. And my father was still praying. My mother's tears, I will never forget. She was only begging to God that her belongings-- the clothes should be saved, and perhaps her little house and a few houses. I was sitting on that wet, damp-- it's September. The grass was damp. We all sat, me and Moishele and Shmulek. Zisman with Leibl were on the farm. They were afraid when the Germans will come in they take young men. So my father sent them to a farmer, a Jewish farmer, to hide over there. And, of course, kids, they cannot do no harm. So we stayed with our parents. And we are sitting next to our parents and watching the smoke, without a stop a whole night. The morning arrived-- Saturday morning. It lingered down. The smoke was less and less. We could see the sky clear. We picked ourselves up in the morning. But on a Friday night, when it started to get in the evening, I will never forget. My mother took out the candles, and she put them in the Earth. And she lit them, and she said the prayer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my father said the blessing over that challah. [SOBBING] And he-- he-- he took a piece of challah off and gave it to Moishele, and to Shmulek, and to me, and to my mother. And he says, let's eat. And we sat the whole night, OK? Couldn't wait for that night to go away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was in a sh-- with-- up to here, you know-- in a cotton little dress-- September. I didn't expect-- it's a house little dress I was wearing. We couldn't wait for the morning. The morning arrived. We picked ourselves up, anxiously, to go back to the shtetl and see what happened. We came back. We could not walk in our shtetl of Wachock. Everything was burnt down, all the houses only smoldering. Ruins were still smoldering, you know, with smoke. And the sidewalk was so hot, I felt dizzy. I couldn't-- the-- the sidewalk was so hot that I felt through my shoes. And my mother stood there, and she says, [NON-ENGLISH]. The candle-- the candelabra [INAUDIBLE] was-- were bent and rusted in the ruins. I says, mama. She says to Shmulek, go pick them up. I says Mama, don't pick them up. They're bent. They're burnt. We are going to get new ones. Leave them. Because it was still hot and burning, like, the wood from the house and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: One miracle happened in that city. What was the miracle? The only miracle, what was saved, was our synagogue. Why was the synagogue-- you call a miracle? It was a miracle for us, because we could at least go in for the few days and be-- this was our shelter. The miracle-- the synagogue was about a half a mile away from the city, from the shtetl. And it was a little bridge. And a little river went through. So because of the water, they didn't got the connection to get-- they didn't get-- the Germans didn't cross that little bridge to burn the synagogue. You know? So they just were concerned to burn all the houses around the city in the Jewish community. The non-Jewish houses were not burned.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first one, there were-- the Jewish people lived. But further down-- like, we call it, like, residential strictly for the gentiles-- they didn't go down-- further down, but just is the mark where the Jewish people lived and the beginning from the-- from the streets, you know? The Jewish people were occupied, too. They had their homes. Somebody came running that the synagogue is still there, wasn't burned. So we picked up ourselves-- all the people that were-- you know, with the children-- all- all the habitants from our shtetl. And we went to that synagogue. We sat down on these benches from that synagogue. We looked around. The mothers were hitting their heads in the wall and praying. They didn't have no food for the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: The children were crying unbelievable. There were no water, because it was-- no water-- where to take? Everything was burned. From food? Forget about it. It was nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: We sat there. I didn't cry. I was-- I was-- I don't know. I was not normal. I left the crying to my mother. I looked at her tears, and she cried.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all of these mothers cried and cried. And the children, the small ones they're holding in their arms, were crying. They wanted a little water. What did they know? They would ask for water. How long did you stay in the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed there for three days. My brother Moishe--

speaker: interviewer
text: And about how many people were in the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there were about 100 families and beside the children. And we stayed outside, too, outside in the synagogue, sitting on the ground. It was September. It was warm. At night, we came into the synagogue. We all got together, standing and sitting, whatever. We gave the privilege to the small children to lay down on these benches from the synagogue. And my brother Moishe-- after the second day, my brother Moishe was by river. He went to dig some potatoes, to make a fire outside and take the water from the river, you know, and dig out a bent pot. And we boiled some potatoes. Because you know, it was unbelievable. And we ate-- we ate some of these potatoes with the-- was cooked in the river-- the water from the river. And after three days, sitting like that, outside, with my parents, near the synagogue, a man walked over to us-- a Jewish man. And he says he came from another city with a horse and wagon. He got stuck in another city. He didn't know the Germans will come with such a force. And he says, he's going to Suchedniow where my sister lives. So my parents, my mother begged him. If you're going to Suchedniow, perhaps you can take my Rachela to my older daughter. Because I see the girl cannot take it. She's falling apart. Take her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Moishele was staying with Shmulek. My mother, later, picked herself up. And she went, also, by horse and wagon. Because the war stopped in a week, you know? In 10 days, it was-- all the shtetls, all the cities were taken. Only Warsaw had put to up a fight. You know, only Warsaw took longer.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the small cities, they didn't put a fight. They just marched in. The Germans, they marched in. They didn't stay there in our city. They just marched through to go to Warsaw to fight. And they were standing. They were eating their sandwiches, you know. They were throwing down the half of the cigarettes. You know, some men should pick it up from the Earth. They were so mean. I looked at them. I could not believe, as a young girl. I was 15. They were so handsome-looking. And they had the smile on their faces, perhaps looking at us. That they could be so mean and so cruel, to do and to obey and to have a heart, you know, to do to humanity-- to people-- like they would not have themselves children, if they would not have wives or parents, they would only be themselves. But, of course, they had parents. And some of them, they had wives and children. But they were so cruel. I could not believe that that time, that world and people could obey and be so cruel. Could not understand. My mind could not, you know, understand that. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to Suchedniow with--

speaker: interviewee
text: I went for the whole day it took me. I came into Suchedniow. I was so hungry. The man gave me a piece of hard bread. He asked me many questions. I wasn't-- I was too weak to talk to him. I was shivering with my dress. He gave me some old rags to cover myself. I felt so dizzy-- bumpa-ba-bumpa-- going with that horse, with that, you know-- with that wagon. I came to Suchedniow. Thank God, I am here. Thinking back on that wagon, I just had left my sister, because I went for school vacation before the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was in her house. She took me to the train to come back. Here, I am again in her house? And that's the way-- with that news to bring her. And this is-- I didn't expect that. I expected to go back home and attend school. And my parents will send me to Krakow. And then that horror? I could not believe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was so depressed. I was so down to myself. He says-- the man-- this is the number your sister lives. This is the house. I says, yes, I know. She lives upstairs.

speaker: interviewer
text: I came upstairs holding my hands, you know? Hands-- covering my hands, like that. I knocked at her door. And my sister goes, opens the door.

speaker: interviewee
text: When she saw me, she says, oh, my god-- the only words what she could say. She understood what happened. She looked at me. I was with bruises. I was all with dust my face. My hair had long braids. Knotted up, my hair. She said, oh, my god. I says, everything is OK. And she started to be panic and cry. I says, don't cry. We are alive. We only lost everything, but we are alive. She sat down for a few minutes, and holding her head down. And she says, oh, my god. Oh, my god. But I stayed in my sister's house for six months until my parents left to Skarzysko after five days staying in Wachock. Because my father had a sister, Hindi, with [INAUDIBLE], his brother-in-law. So he went. He says, perhaps, that was his sister. That was the first step to get to her house. So the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they had a chance to get there. And Moishele-- my mother, like I mentioned, went with the younger son to her parents, to Radoszyce. And Moishele, with Berl, with my brother, and Zisman went to Hindi. And Leibl-- Zisman came back from the farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: After they occupied Wachock, the Germans, he came back. And he wanted to see what's doing. The farm wasn't far away. And Leibl stayed on the farm. He stayed on the farm. And he didn't want to come back, because, he says, so many people [INAUDIBLE]. Where will he fit in?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the farm, his name was Schmel. He had a big farm. And he had four sons with a daughter. Her name was Rivkele. She was a young girl at the age of 17. My brother at that time was 19, in 19-- in 1939-- 19. And then, of course, he didn't think about it. But he offered Schmel that he's going to teach the children, and then to get more children from the farm-- around the farm. And he could teach many children. And he wanted to, you know, to help them out. With that, he will have a home. And he will have food. Because he didn't want to come home in a situation like that and to make life more harder for his aunt and uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: So but that time, until my aunt got us an apartment-- in a ghetto apartment, of course, where only Jewish people lived, it was just two little, small, little rooms. A little kitchen, with a small little room she could fit in a table with two chairs. That's all. That was the second room.

speaker: interviewee
text: The kitchen was one little bed, you know, on the side and a stove. And the table was in the other room. That was our ghetto apartment. But happy to have that. We didn't have nothing. My aunt provided us with pillows. She asked other people in the city, whoever had enough of that merchandise-- you know, like blankets or-- her friend had extra blankets and pillows. She asked them if she could get it from them and help us. Well, Jewish people always were considerate for each other, to help each other. That was the part of life in a situation like that, specially in the war. But during that time, while we had a ghetto apartment, Leibl, the scholar, the brilliant boy, when he was surrounded with a young, pretty girl, Rivkele, he fell in love with her. My parents were furious. The war was on. A farmer, for us, was below dignity-- below dignity-- below our stature.

speaker: interviewer
text: My mother told Leibl the scholar, Lublin Yeshiva, should wait for the war to stop and marry the right girl. You know, what is the rush in a war in that age? He was only that age.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I would never forget when I was young then. When he came home begging my mother to give him the permission to get married to Rivkele, he says, ma-- he didn't speak to father. He didn't tell his father. He told my mother. And I was standing and listening. He says, ma, you don't understand what means love-- liebe-- love. I love Rivkele in my heart. I love her with my whole soul. I would give my whole life for her. The war wouldn't last long. Soon the war will come to an end. But ma, try to understand that I love this girl. My life would be miserable without her. I could not meet another girl, never in my life, and not love her like I love her.

speaker: interviewer
text: My mother says to him, [NON-ENGLISH], you are too young. [NON-ENGLISH]. And you don't know what means a war. And who knows how the war will last? When it-- when it-- how long it will go on? The First World War lasted for years. And who knows how many years this will last? And what would you do if you get married? You have such a big responsibility.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. Listen to me. Try to understand. I'm trying just to tell you. He says, ma, dear. He left our home-- our ghetto home. And he put his hand on my mother's shoulder. And he says, ma, dear, I cannot help it. My heart would not let me do any different things. He went back, and he got married. Not right away, but he waited. He got married in 1941. My mother and my father, they went to the chuppah. They didn't go to the chuppah! I'm sorry. There was a ghetto already. They couldn't leave the ghetto. But perhaps if they could get a permit, they would. But they didn't. They were afraid. If he wants to get married, let him get married.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in the ghetto? In--

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed in the ghetto for three years, from 1939 to 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the whole time in this apartment that your aunt got you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole time in this apartment. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't work. Girls didn't work. Of course, some girls went to work, cleaning-- to do cleaning for the Germans. I didn't. I didn't. This wasn't a must. That was just, you know, if you wanted to do, you know? If you-- that was a Jewish committee in-- we had a Jewish-- right away it formed the Jewish committee. That was the first step in ghetto. They seeked, looked, people who wanted to work for the Germans, you know, to clean their cars, to do the cooking, or to-- to-- to clean their houses, whatever. But this was when the ghetto was open. It was an open ghetto, which for the first six months, we-- six months, we could travel. We could travel from city to city. People, you know, they were occupied making black-market, you know, business. And life-- we-- people needed-- you know, we needed food. We needed to survive. But after six months--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father work at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: He did. Not too much. He did. But it was very, very difficult, you know? After the ghetto was closed, no Jews could leave the ghetto. It was forbidden to walk from-- we could only walk from 6:00 in the morning to 7:00 in the evening. Then it was forbidden to go out on the street. But for us, you could not leave the ghetto. You have to stay between your vicinity. And, of course, you could go visit your friends, but everything in the ghetto. Ghetto life was ups and downs, I will say. Certain people who had merchandise, they made black market with the Poles. Like, a Pole will sneak in, you know, from his territory to the ghetto. And he will bring in some food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some Jewish people will sneak out and take off the armband and make believe that they are Christians and ride on the train, and so and so and take a risk in their life, you know? So you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And to bring in some food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were already wearing armbands, at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, yeah. The first law, after they-- after six months of the-- the Germans came in, they-- the committee. The committee was everything in our city. The first thing was for the Jews to wear the armband. The second law was for the Jews to cut off the beards. No man should wear-- have a beard. The third law--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to stop now.
